text: The immune system does not operate in isolation from the nervous system. During genuine rest, the vagus nerve activates the cholinergic anti-inflammatory pathway — a circuit discovered by immunologist Kevin Tracey in 2000 — in which acetylcholine suppresses the production of tumor necrosis factor and other pro-inflammatory cytokines in the spleen. This means rest is not passive recuperation but an active immunological operation. Tracey's finding overturned a long-standing assumption that the nervous and immune systems were separate bureaucracies. Sustained sympathetic activation — the physiological signature of chronic stress — keeps this anti-inflammatory circuit offline, which helps explain epidemiological links between chronic stress and inflammatory disease.
question: What did Kevin Tracey's 2000 discovery reveal about the vagus nerve and inflammation?

options.text: The vagus nerve activates a cholinergic anti-inflammatory pathway that suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokine production
The vagus nerve triggers cytokine release during acute stress to fight infection
The vagus nerve coordinates white blood cell production in the spleen during exercise
The vagus nerve signals the adrenal glands to reduce cortisol after an immune response
